elements.sent-2411-001: Characterizing the nonclassicality of quantum systems under minimal assumptions is an important challenge for quantum foundations and technology.
elements.sent-2411-002: Here we introduce a theory-independent method of process tomography and perform it on a superconducting qubit.
elements.sent-2411-003: We demonstrate its decoherence without assuming quantum theory or trusting the devices by modelling the system as a general probabilistic theory.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that the superconducting system is initially well-described as a quantum bit, but that its realized state space contracts over time, which in quantum terminology indicates its loss of coherence.
elements.sent-2411-005: The system is initially nonclassical in the sense of generalized contextuality: it does not admit of a hidden-variable model where statistically indistinguishable preparations are represented by identical hidden-variable distributions.
elements.sent-2411-006: In finite time, the system becomes noncontextual and hence loses its nonclassicality.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, we demonstrate in a theory-independent way that the system undergoes non-Markovian evolution at late times.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results extend theory-independent tomography to time-evolving systems, and show how important dynamical physical phenomena can be experimentally monitored without assuming quantum theory.
elements.sent-2411-009: Can the predictions of quantum physics be explained classically, in terms of some underlying hidden-variable model? Answering this question in the negative, under natural assumptions and for concrete physical systems, is the goal of foundational and technological research.
elements.sent-2411-010: The strongest form of nonclassicality, Bell nonlocality~\cite{Bell , allows us to refute local hidden-variable models for experiments on spatially separated quantum systems.}
elements.sent-2411-011: It is often experimentally more tractable to focus on single systems, and the Kochen-Specker theorem~\cite{KochenSpecker precludes the existence of noncontextual hidden-variable models for such systems of Hilbert space dimension at least three.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The Kochen-Specker theorem relies, however, on several undesirable assumptions: it assumes the validity of quantum theory, the fact that the performed measurements are noiseless projective measurements, and that the outcomes depend deterministically on the underlying hidden variables.
elements.sent-2411-013: The latter two assumptions in particular are detrimental to the goal of the practical certification of nonclassicality.
elements.sent-2411-014: Spekkens' notion of generalized noncontextuality overcomes these difficulties~\cite{Spekkens2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In a nutshell, it stipulates that statistically indistinguishable operations (measurements, preparations or transformations) should have identical representations on the hidden-variable level.
elements.sent-2411-016: Generalized contextuality admits of robust experimental detection~\cite{Mazurek2016,Kunjwal,Krishna , and subsumes a variety of natural notions of nonclassicality (such as Wigner negativity~\cite{Spekkens2008 ) into a single simple criterion.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Here, we certify the generalized contextuality of a superconducting qubit experimentally, directly from the measurement statistics and without assuming quantum theory.
elements.sent-2411-018: In contrast to earlier experimental detection of Kochen-Specker contextuality in a superconducting platform~\cite{Jerger , our analysis thus makes significantly weaker assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-019: We analyze how the amount of contextuality changes over time --- in particular, the system becomes noncontextual in finite time due to decoherence.
elements.sent-2411-020: In fact, we do more than this: we characterize the system completely, for different evolution times, by generalizing theory-agnostic tomography~\cite{Mazurek2021,Grabowecky2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: That is, by performing many preparation and measurement procedures, we determine its space of states and of effects (measurement outcomes) which, when combined linearly, reproduce the experimental statistics.
elements.sent-2411-022: Such pairs are known as generalized probabilistic theories~\cite{Barrett2007,Janotta,Mueller,Plavala , with quantum theory and its qubit as a special case.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For a quantum bit, the state space is the three-dimensional Bloch ball, and this is reproduced to good accuracy in our experiment.
elements.sent-2411-024: Observing the contraction of this ``bumpy Bloch ball'' yields a theory-independent monitoring of the quantum decoherence process, and this process turns out to feature non-Markovianity~\cite{Li at late times.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Superconducting qubits~\cite{Kjaergaard admit the rapid implementation of a very large number of preparation and measurement procedures, which is necessary for the data-demanding sort of process tomography that we implement here.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Our results hence demonstrate the suitability of the superconducting system for this sort of theory-independent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our experiments are performed on a transmon superconducting qubit~\cite{Koch with frequency $\omega_q=2\pi\cdot\unit{5.05 {GHz}$, hosted inside a three-dimensional readout cavity resonator.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Strong dispersive coupling between the two allows us to measure the qubit's state by performing a transmission measurement on the resonator to probe its qubit-state-dependent resonance frequency, as standard in circuit-QED~\cite{BlaisRMP .}
elements.sent-2411-029: This signal is amplified by both cryogenic and room temperature amplifiers, which give a read out fidelity of $85(1) \%$.
elements.sent-2411-030: We measure a qubit energy relaxation rate $T_1=\unit{21.9 \pm 0.4 {\mu s}$ and Ramsey decoherence rate $T_2^\ast=\unit{12.7 \pm 0.6 {\mu s}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Preparation of the initial state is achieved by applying a $\unit{200 {ns}$ resonant Rabi pulse with well-defined amplitude and phase to the qubit $t=0$ state $\ket{0}$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This amplitude and phase are chosen from a list $\mathcal{V _m$ defining the space of $m$ states.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In order to have a uniform distribution of $m$ points on the surface of the Bloch sphere, we chose a Fibonacci distribution~\cite{Gonzalez .}
elements.sent-2411-034: We then wait for a variable time $\tau$, after which a second Rabi pulse is applied in order to specify the measurement basis.
elements.sent-2411-035: Again, amplitude and phase of the pulse are chosen from a list $\mathcal{V _n$, now specifying the space of effects.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In our case, $m=n=100$, and the two lists of preparations and measurement directions coincide.
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, the qubit's state is measured in the computational $z$-basis $\{\ket{0 ,\ket{1}\}$ by the standard circuit-QED dispersive read-out technique mentioned above.}
elements.sent-2411-038: For each combination of state preparation and measurement, this sequence is repeated $2000$ times in order to average over the qubit projection noise.
elements.sent-2411-039: This gives us the frequencies $F_{ij}$ from which we estimate the probabilities $p(0|P_i,M_j)$ entering Eq.~\eqref{eq:D below.}
elements.sent-2411-040: To provide a theory-independent analysis, we view the experimental setup as a prepare-and-measure scenario, see \Cref{fig:prepare-and-measure .}
elements.sent-2411-041: We have $m$ possible preparation procedures $P_i$ and $n$ measurement procedures $M_j$, each with outcome $a\in\{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Moreover, we have the option to introduce a waiting time $\tau$ between preparation and measurement.
elements.sent-2411-043: Without loss of generality, we choose the convention that this evolution for time $\tau$ is considered a part of the preparation procedure, yielding effective preparation procedures $P_i^\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Thus, we probe the system (the superconducting qubit) to estimate the conditional probabilities $p(a|P_i^\tau,M_j)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: By normalization, it is sufficient to obtain the statistics for one of the outcomes which we denote by $a=0$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Statistics are gathered for all the possible $i,j$ and for $\tau=\{0,5,10,15,20,30,40,50\}$ $\mu$s.
elements.sent-2411-047: For every fixed value of $\tau$, this defines a corresponding operational theory~\cite{Spekkens2005 : a collection of preparation and measurement procedures together with a probability rule that describe the statistical properties of the laboratory system.}

elements.sent-2411-048: Following this operational approach, generalized noncontextuality~\cite{Spekkens2005 requires that procedures which are statistically equivalent at the operational level (i.e., producing identical statistics if all other processes are fixed) must also be identically represented in the underlying classical model.}
elements.sent-2411-049: A classical model (also known as an ontological model or hidden variable model) of an operational theory is given by a measurable space $\Lambda$ corresponding to the set of deterministic classical states.
elements.sent-2411-050: To each preparation $P$ in the theory, there corresponds a probability distribution $\mu_P(\lambda)$ on $\Lambda$, and to every outcome $a$ of every measurement $M$, there corresponds a response function $\chi_{a|M (\lambda)$, such that $\sum_a \chi_{a|M (\lambda) = 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The assignments are such that the probabilities are reproduced by the laws of classical probability theory, \begin{equation}\label{eq:prob_decomp} p(a|P,M)=\int_\Lambda \chi_{a|M}(\lambda)\mu_P(\lambda){\rm d}\lambda.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-052: A classical model is preparation noncontextual~\cite{Spekkens2005 if any two operationally equivalent preparations $P$ and $P'$ have $\mu_{P (\lambda) = \mu_{P' (\lambda)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Two preparations $P$ and $P'$ are operationally equivalent if \begin{equation}p(a|P, M) = p(a|P', M) for all a,M.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}p(a|P, M) = p(a|P', M) for all a,M.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-054: Similarly, the model is measurement noncontextual if operationally equivalent measurement outcomes (i.e., ones that have identical probabilities for all preparation procedures) are represented by the same response function.
elements.sent-2411-055: For our scenario, we may not only consider the actually implemented procedures $M_i$ and $P_j$, but also statistical mixtures of those such as $\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda)P_2$, where $0\leq\lambda\leq 1$ is the probability of implementing procedure $P_1$ as opposed to $P_2$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We can then represent operationally equivalent preparation procedures $P$ and $P'$ by some state $\vec s_P=\vec s_{P'}$, an element of some vector space over the real numbers.
elements.sent-2411-057: Similarly, we can represent operationally equivalent measurement-outcome pairs $(a,M)$ and $(a',M')$ as so-called effects $\vec e_{a,M =\vec e_{a',M'}$, elements of the dual space, such that $p(a|P,M)=\langle \vec e_{a,M ,\vec s_P \rangle$.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: All possible states of a system form a convex set $\mathcal{S}$, and all possible effects yield another convex set $\mathcal{E}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: This defines a generalized probabilistic theory (GPT)~\cite{Mueller,Plavala , sometimes also called a ``GPT system''.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Please see the Methods section for more details, and for how a quantum bit is described in this formalism.
elements.sent-2411-061: In particular, its space of pure states is the famous Bloch sphere, and the full space $\mathcal{S}$ of all (pure and mixed) states is the Bloch ball.
elements.sent-2411-062: Quantum systems and classical random variables are examples of GPT systems, but the framework contains many more exotic possibilities, such as theories with superstrong nonlocality~\cite{Barrett2007 and higher-order interference~\cite{UdudecBarnumEmerson,BarnumMuellerUdudec .}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Originally, interest in GPTs originated in the research program to reconstruct the abstract formalism of quantum theory from simple physical principles~\cite{Hardy,DakicBrukner,MasanesMueller,Chiribella .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Here, however, we use the GPT formalism as a tool for a theory-independent description of our experimental statistics.
elements.sent-2411-065: In particular, knowing the GPT associated to an operational theory allows us to determine whether it admits of a preparation- and measurement-noncontextual classical model, using the criterion of simplex embeddability demonstrated in~\cite{Schmid-Characterization .}
elements.sent-2411-066: If it does admit of such a model, we say that the physical system is noncontextual.
elements.sent-2411-067: This can be checked with a linear program~\cite{Gitton-solvable,Selby-LinearProgram .}
elements.sent-2411-068: For example, it can be checked that the qubit GPT system is contextual, i.e.\ cannot be embedded into any classical probability simplex, in contrast to the stabilizer qubit, a GPT system defined by restricting the qubit to convex combinations of its stabilizer states and to stabilizer measurements~\cite{lilystone_contextuality_2019 , as illustrated in~\Cref{fig:stabilizer_qubit .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Hence, the stabilizer qubit is noncontextual (when restricted to preparations and measurements) or ``classical'' in this sense.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is related to the Gottesman-Knill theorem, establishing the efficient classical simulatability of stabilizer quantum computation~\cite{GottesmanKnill .}
elements.sent-2411-071: More generally, generalized contextuality is a resource for a number of information processing tasks~\cite{Spekkens_preparation_2009, Hameedi_communication_2017,Duarte_resource_2018,Saha_preparation_2019,Catani_resource_2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-072: The obstacle to applying this directly to our experiment is that we do not know the probabilities $p(a|P,M)$, but only experimentally determined approximations, namely the frequencies of occurrence of the outcomes (depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:frequency_data ).}
elements.sent-2411-073: Hence, we cannot directly write down the GPT that describes our superconducting system, but have to estimate the GPT from the experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-074: We do so by modifying and generalizing the method of theory-agnostic tomography~\cite{Mazurek2021,Grabowecky2022 , which leads to the following multi-step procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Let us first discuss the procedure for a fixed value of waiting time $\tau$; say, $\tau=0$.
elements.sent-2411-076: We start by collecting the experimental statistics for a large set of possible preparations $P_i$ and two-outcome measurements $M_j$, with $i\in\{1,\ldots,m\}$ and $j\in\{1,\ldots,n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-077: The goal is to estimate the conditional probabilities $p(0|P_i,M_j)$ for obtaining outcome $a=0$, given preparation $P_i$ and measurement $M_j$.
elements.sent-2411-078: We organize the collection of statistics in the form of an $m \times n$ matrix $D$ as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:D} D=\left(\begin{array}{cccc} p(0|P_1,M_1) p(0|P_1,M_2) \cdots p(0|P_1,M_n) p(0|P_2,M_1) p(0|P_2,M_2) \cdots p(0|P_2,M_n) \vdots \cdots \ddots \vdots p(0|P_m,M_1) p(0|P_m,M_2) \cdots p(0|P_m,M_n) \end{array}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:D} D=\left({cccc} p(0|P_1,M_1) p(0|P_1,M_2) \cdots p(0|P_1,M_n) p(0|P_2,M_1) p(0|P_2,M_2) \cdots p(0|P_2,M_n) \vdots \cdots \ddots \vdots p(0|P_m,M_1) p(0|P_m,M_2) \cdots p(0|P_m,M_n) \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-079: In an actual experiment, the conditional probabilities $p(0|P_i,M_j)$ are estimated by running the experiment $N$ times.
elements.sent-2411-080: Thus, when talking about experimental data, instead of the matrix $D$ we will refer to a matrix $F$ containing the observed frequencies $F_{ij =f(0|P_i,M_j)$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Given that there are $N$ runs for each pair of preparation and measurement $(P_i, M_j)$, we have $F_{ij = \frac{N_{ij {N}$, where $N_{ij}$ is the number of outcomes $0$ observed for the pair $(P_i, M_j)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In the case where a large number $M$ of frequency tables $\{F^q\}_{q=1 ^M$ are obtained, one can directly compute the variance of a frequency table $F^q$ as $(\Delta F_{ij ^q)^2 = (F_{ij ^q - \bar F_{ij )^2$, where $\bar F_{ij = \frac{1 {M \sum_{q =1 ^M F_{ij ^q$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In the case where one does not have a large number of frequency tables, we can estimate $(\Delta F_{ij )^2$ by making the following assumptions~\cite{Mazurek2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We expect that in the limit of $N\rightarrow \infty$, these frequencies converge to the conditional probabilities $p(0|P_i,M_j)$, under the assumption of ideal repeatability disregarding drift and other imperfections.
elements.sent-2411-086: In this case, the variable $N_{ij}$ is the number of $0$ outcomes of $N$ independent events in a sequence of experiments.

elements.sent-2411-098: The best rank $k$ matrix $D$ is determined via minimization of the weighted $\chi_k^2$ in the following optimization problem~\cite{Mazurek2021 : \begin{equation}\label{eq:chisquared} \begin{array}{rl} \min\limits_{D \in M_{mn}} \chi^2_k=\sum\limits_{ij} \frac{(F_{ij}-D_{ij})^2}{\Delta F_{ij}^2} subject to rank(D)=k,\, 0\leq D_{ij}\leq 1. \end{array}\end{equation} In the Methods Subsection~\ref{sec:optimization_prob , we describe how we solve this nonconvex optimization problem numerically, following the method presented in~\cite{Mazurek2021 .}}}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Solving the weighted low-rank approximation problem of~\Cref{eq:chisquared}
elements.sent-2411-252: In this section, we outline the methodology introduced in~\cite{Mazurek2021 to find a rank-$k$ matrix $D^{realized}$, which serves as the best estimate for the ideal probability table $D$ leading to the experimentally observed statistics $F$.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Let us recall, that this is achieved by minimizing the weighted $\chi^2_k$ statistics in the following optimization problem~(\ref{eq:chisquared ): \begin{equation}\begin{array}{rl} \min\limits_{D \in M_{mn}} \chi^2_k=\sum\limits_{ij} \frac{(F_{ij}-D_{ij})^2}{\Delta F_{ij}^2} subject to rank(D)=k,\, 0\leq D_{ij}\leq 1, \nonumber \end{array}\end{equation} where $\Delta F_{ij ^2$ is the statistical variance in $F_{ij}$.}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}{rl} \min\limits_{D \in M_{mn}} \chi^2_k=\sum\limits_{ij} \frac{(F_{ij}-D_{ij})^2}{\Delta F_{ij}^2} subject to rank(D)=k,\, 0\leq D_{ij}\leq 1, \nonumber \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-254: Note that $D=SE$ contains the inner products $\vec{s ^\top_i \vec{e _j$, which makes Eq.~\eqref{eq:chisquared a non-convex optimization problem.}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Moreover, this minimization problem is known to not have an analytical solution~\cite{Low-Rank-Matrix, Markovsky-LowRankMatrix .}
elements.sent-2411-256: Following~\cite{Mazurek2021 , we employ an iterative approach that decomposes the optimization into two convex subproblems.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Specifically, we alternate between optimizing the states while fixing the effects and optimizing the effects while fixing the states, with each optimization being a convex quadratic program~\cite{Mazurek2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-258: This iterative process, known as the see-saw algorithm~\cite{PalVertesi-seesaw, WernerWolf-seesaw , continues until the cost function, here the $\chi_k^2$, converges to a desired numerical precision.}
elements.sent-2411-259: However, it is important to note that due to the non-convexity of the general problem, this procedure does not guarantee convergence to a global minimum.
elements.sent-2411-260: We refer the reader to~\cite[Appendix C]{Mazurek2021 for an explicit description of how the $\chi^2_k$ minimization in Eq.~\eqref{eq:chisquared can be translated into a series of alternating convex optimizations.}}
elements.sent-2411-261: The output of the optimization algorithm is a rank $k$ matrix $D$ with entries in $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-262: In order to obtain the corresponding GPT states and effects, we must factor $D$ into two matrices $S$ and $E$.
elements.sent-2411-263: Note that this factorization is non-unique.
elements.sent-2411-264: As in~\cite{Mazurek2021 , we first append a column of 1's to $D$, which reflects the probability associated with the unit effect (corresponding to the identity operator in quantum theory), assuming no experimental losses.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Then, as described in~\cite[Appendix C]{Mazurek2021 , we perform a QR decomposition of $D$ to express it as $D = SE$.}
elements.sent-2411-266: This ensures that the first column of $S$ consists of 1's, thus effectively encoding the normalization of the GPT states (analogous to the trace degree of freedom for quantum states).
elements.sent-2411-267: Hence the matrices of states and effects have the following forms: \begin{equation}S =  1 s_1^1 \hdots s_1^{k-1} 1 s_2^1 \hdots s_2^{k-1} \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots 1 s_m^1 \hdots \hdots s_m^{k-1}  =  \vec s_1^\top \vec s_2^\top \vdots \vec s_m^\top  , E =  1 e^0_1 \hdots e_n^{0} 0 e^1_1 \hdots e_n^{1} \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots 0 e^{k-1}_1 \hdots e^{k-1}_n  =  \vec e_1 \vec e_2 \hdots \vec e_n  .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}S =  1 s_1^1 \hdots s_1^{k-1} 1 s_2^1 \hdots s_2^{k-1} \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots 1 s_m^1 \hdots \hdots s_m^{k-1}  =  \vec s_1^\top \vec s_2^\top \vdots \vec s_m^\top  , E =  1 e^0_1 \hdots e_n^{0} 0 e^1_1 \hdots e_n^{1} \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots 0 e^{k-1}_1 \hdots e^{k-1}_n  =  \vec e_1 \vec e_2 \hdots \vec e_n  .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-268: From the optimization problem and choice of factorization, we obtain the unit effect $\mathbf{1 = \vec e_0$ and $n$ effects $\vec e_1\dots \vec e_n$, where $\vec s_i^\top \vec e_j\in[0,1]$ for all $i,j$ by construction.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Every measurement $M_j$ has two outcomes $0$ and $1$ with $ P(0|P_i,M_j) = \vec s_i^\top \vec e_j$.
elements.sent-2411-270: Since $P(0|P_i, M_j) +P(1|P_i, M_j) = 1 $ it follows that $P(1|P_i, M_j) = 1 - s_i^\top e_j = s_i^\top (\mathbf{1 - e_j) $.}
elements.sent-2411-271: Hence in order to account for the probabilities $P(1|P_i, M_j)$ the effect space should include the complement effect $(\mathbf{1 - \vec e_j)$ for every $\vec e_j$.}
elements.sent-2411-272: We append the $(n+1) \times k$ matrix of complement effects $E'$ with columns $(\mathbf{1 - \vec e_j)$ to the matrix $E$ to obtain a $2(n+1) \times k$ matrix of effects (which we shall label as $E$ hereon for convenience).}
elements.sent-2411-273: Augmenting the set of effects in this manner does not change the set of consistent states or effects.
elements.sent-2411-274: When solving Eq.~\eqref{eq:chisquared , note that as the chosen rank $k$ increases, the error of the fit $\chi^2_k$ naturally decreases.}
elements.sent-2411-275: However, note that in general the experimental noise causes $F$ to be a full rank matrix, even when the underlying $D$ it approximates is not.
elements.sent-2411-276: Therefore, increasing $k$ can result in a model that overfits the experimental data, which is to say that it fits the noise in the training set data $F$.
elements.sent-2411-277: In practice, this can be determined by separating the data into a training set $F=F^{train}$ and a test set $F'=F^{test}$, where the ${\chi^2_k ^{test}$ is evaluated according to~\Cref{eq:chisquared_test .}}
elements.sent-2411-278: The ${\chi^2_k ^{train}$ for the training data determines how much the model underfits the data; the higher the ${\chi^2_k}$ value the worse the fit is.}
elements.sent-2411-279: To test for overfitting, the model is applied to the test set $F^{test}$.
elements.sent-2411-280: Models which overfit will have a significantly worse ${\chi^2_k ^{test}$ since they will fit to noise which was present in $F^{train}$ but is not present in $F^{train}$.}
elements.sent-2411-281: There is a value $k^{opt}$ for which the models will transition from underfitting to overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-282: Then, the matrix $D_{k^{\text{opt }$ is chosen as the best fit.}}
elements.sent-2411-283: In summary, to estimate the rank $k^{opt}$, we hypothesize several values $k$ for $k^{opt}$ and test each one.
elements.sent-2411-284: Then, for each hypothesized rank, we compute the optimal estimate of the rank-$k$ matrix $D$ and evaluate its fit to the data using the $\chi^2$ statistic.
elements.sent-2411-285: Additionally, to decide which $k$ provides the best balance between fitting the experimental data and maintaining simplicity in the model, we split the analysis with a training and a test set to ensure that the model is not overfitting the data.
elements.sent-2411-286: As we have seen in Subsection~\ref{MethodsGPTs , if the experiment is described by a qubit quantum model (as we expect, since we have prepared and probed a system that is, from quantum physics and the experimental setup, expected to be a qubit), then the rank is expected to be $4$.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Indeed, following this method we have shown in~\Cref{fig:OverUnderFittingRank that this is the case.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Nonetheless, recall that we are keeping it general to follow a theory-agnostic approach, where we obtain the best GPT fit without assuming the correctness of quantum theory.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Determining the set of consistent states

elements.sent-2411-333: Having shown the image of the GPT state space according to the time delay $\tau$, we want a theory-independent test of their (non-)contextuality.
elements.sent-2411-334: It was shown in~\cite{Gitton-solvable that non-contextuality can be established via a linear program which takes as an input a set of states and a set of effects, and determines whether their statistics, via a specified probability rule, can be reproduced by a classical (noncontextual) model.}
elements.sent-2411-335: We make use of an open-source version of this program~\cite{Selby-LinearProgram , which, moreover, in the case that the GPT is contextual, computes the amount of noise that must be added such that a noncontextual model can be fitted.}
elements.sent-2411-336: More specifically, the linear program of~\cite{Selby-LinearProgram is a test of simplex embeddability: the property that a GPT's state space can be embedded into a classical probability simplex, with its effect space as the dual of that simplex.}
elements.sent-2411-337: This was shown in~\cite{Schmid-Characterization,Selby-AccessibleFragments to be equivalent to the existence of a noncontextual ontological model.}
elements.sent-2411-338: For the nonembeddibility of the GPT to meaningfully imply nonclassicality, we must assume that there is some system that our experiment probes for which our preparations and measurements are relatively tomographically complete~\cite{Schmid2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-339: We note that the system being probed will in general be a subsystem of some larger system, for instance in this case it corresponds to the first two energy levels of an anharmonic oscillator, however crucially it must be such that the preparations and measurement of that subsystem are tomographically complete relative to each other, or equivalently, that the subsystem being probed is embeddable within the larger system~\cite{MuellerGarner .}
elements.sent-2411-340: Without assuming so, it is always possible that the statistics derive from some higher-dimensional, noncontextual system, c.f.\ the Holevo projection~\cite{Holevo .}
elements.sent-2411-341: Tomographic completeness in itself is a theory-independent assumption, but can be strongly motivated here by quantum physics --- in particular, by the state space of the qubit being a Bloch ball, for which we have chosen a tomographically highly overcomplete distribution of points to sample ($k=4$ linearly independent states and effects would have been sufficient for tomographic completeness, but many more of these procedures are needed to obtain an accurate estimate of the shape of the state and effect spaces as depicted in Figures~\ref{fig:rank4stateseffects and~\ref{fig:decoherence_plots ).}}
elements.sent-2411-342: To use the linear program, one inputs the GPT's set of states {$\mathcal{S ^\tau$} and set of effects $\mathcal{E}$, which were found via the theory-independent analysis of Section~\ref{subsec:theoryindependent_analysis , as well as two row vectors specifying the unit effect $\vec u$ and the average state $\vec m^\tau$.}}
elements.sent-2411-343: The unit effect is given by $(1,0,0,0)$, which is always the first entry of the set of effects, whilst the average state is calculated by taking the columnwise mean of the set of states.
elements.sent-2411-344: The program then outputs a coefficient $r$, termed the robustness of nonclassicality, as well as a set of epistemic states and response functions (which specify a noncontextual ontological model for the scenario).
elements.sent-2411-345: The important information is encoded in $r$, which quantifies how much depolarizing noise must be added such that a noncontextual ontological model can be fitted.
elements.sent-2411-346: In particular, the maximally mixed state defines a depolarization map $D^\tau_r(\vec s):=(1-r)\vec s + r \vec m^\tau$; the minimum $r$ such that a noncontextual ontological model can be fitted to the scenario thus constitutes an operational measure of nonclassicality.
elements.sent-2411-347: More specifically, a robustness of $r=0$ indicates that a noncontextual ontological model can be constructed precisely, without adding noise, whilst $r>0$ indicates that some amount of depolarizing noise is necessary for an ontological model -- thereby witnessing contextuality.
